content.text: LLM deployments have two distinct rollback mechanisms —

(replace the stable model pointer) — and they serve different severity levels. Choosing the wrong one wastes time in an incident. A structured decision tree and pre-written scripts let on-call engineers act in seconds rather than reconstructing the logic under pressure.

attrs.alt: Rollback decision tree: anomaly detected → canary check → severity → flag rollback or model swap

content.text: The fastest rollback is a flag write. During an active canary — when

is between 0 and 1 — setting it back to 0.0 moves 100 % of traffic to the stable model within one TTL cycle (30 s), or under 1 s if the flag store uses pub/sub cache invalidation. This is the first action for any canary incident regardless of severity.

# scripts/rollback.py
"""Flag-based rollback — takes effect within one TTL cycle (~30 s)."""
import sys
from app.flags import flag_store

def flag_rollback(reason: str) -> None:
    flag_store.set('canary_pct', 0.0)          # stop canary traffic
    flag_store.set('new_rag_pipeline', False)   # disable experimental RAG
    flag_store.set('rollback_reason', reason)   # audit trail
    flag_store.set('rollback_at', __import__('time').time())
    print(f'Flag rollback activated: {reason}')
    print('canary_pct=0.0, new_rag_pipeline=False')
    print('Effect propagates within 30 s (TTL) or <1 s (pub/sub).')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    flag_rollback(reason=' '.join(sys.argv[1:]) or 'manual rollback')

Keep this script in the repo and test it in staging monthly. The on-call engineer should be able to execute it from a phone over SSH. Never build a UI rollback button that makes the same Redis write — the script is auditable (Git history), the button is not.
When the canary has already been promoted to 100 % —
has been updated to the new version — a flag write no longer helps because there is no longer a previous version in active use. A model swap is required: update
in the flag store to the previous version and route all traffic there. The critical optimisation is
pre-warming the target version before switching traffic
: keep the previous two model versions loaded in GPU memory at all times so the swap takes 30 s instead of 2 minutes.

# scripts/model_swap.py
"""Hard model rollback — replaces the stable model pointer."""
import sys
from app.flags import flag_store
from app.models import warm_model

def model_rollback(target_version: str, reason: str) -> None:
    current = flag_store.get('stable_model')
    print(f'Swapping stable model: {current} -> {target_version}')

    # Pre-warm the target version before switching traffic
    warm_model(target_version)
    print(f'Model {target_version} warmed.')

    # Atomic flag write — takes effect on next request
    flag_store.set('canary_pct', 0.0)           # kill any active canary
    flag_store.set('stable_model', target_version)
    flag_store.set('rollback_reason', reason)
    flag_store.set('rollback_at', __import__('time').time())
    print(f'stable_model -> {target_version}. Rollback complete.')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Usage: python model_swap.py v1.8.2 'hallucination spike in v1.9.0'
    model_rollback(target_version=sys.argv[1], reason=sys.argv[2])

Maintain a loaded-model registry in the serving process. At any point keep at most three versions in memory: current stable, current canary, and the previous stable. When a fourth version is loaded, evict the oldest. This bounds GPU memory usage while ensuring rollback is always a pointer swap, not a cold load.
Response time during an incident is critical. Know your time budget before an incident happens:
Requires pub/sub invalidation in flag store
Processes expire local cache on next read
Prior version already in GPU memory
Depends on model size and disk I/O speed
Pull image, start container, health check

The gap between a flag write (< 1 s) and a cold model load (8 min) is the reason to invest in pre-warming and pub/sub invalidation. An 8-minute rollback during a P1 safety incident causes real harm; a sub-second rollback does not.
Assign every rollback trigger to a severity level before an incident. The runbook entry for each level must be a sequence of copy-pasteable commands, not a prose description.

# ops/runbook_rollback.md (markdown summary, not executed)
#
# P1 — Active safety violation or data leak
#   1. python scripts/rollback.py 'P1: safety violation'
#   2. python scripts/model_swap.py <prev_stable> 'P1 rollback'
#   3. Page on-call lead immediately
#   4. python scripts/save_rollback_fixtures.py
#
# P2 — Quality regression > 5 pp neg feedback
#   1. python scripts/rollback.py 'P2: quality regression'
#   2. Monitor for 15 min; if stable, run root-cause eval
#   3. python scripts/save_rollback_fixtures.py
#
# P3 — Latency spike, cost overrun
#   1. flag_store.set('max_tokens_override', 256)  # cost circuit breaker
#   2. OR: flag_store.set('canary_pct', 0.0)       # traffic back to stable
#   3. Investigate during business hours
— the model is producing harmful, illegal, or private content. Rollback immediately without waiting for metrics. Page the on-call lead and open a post-mortem.
— negative feedback rate increases > 5 percentage points above the 7-day baseline. Rollback within 15 minutes; investigate during the incident window.
— p95 latency exceeds SLO or per-request cost exceeds budget threshold. Apply a targeted flag fix (lower max_tokens, disable canary) rather than a full model rollback.

Every rollback is a signal that the test suite missed something. Before the model that triggered the rollback is evicted from memory and its requests age out of the database, save the failing cases as eval fixtures so they cannot regress again.

# scripts/save_rollback_fixture.py
"""Save failing requests as regression eval fixtures after a rollback."""
import json
import psycopg2
from pathlib import Path

FIXTURE_DIR = Path('tests/rag/fixtures/regression')

def save_rollback_fixtures(rollback_at: float, window_minutes: int = 60) -> None:
    conn = psycopg2.connect('postgresql://localhost/llmops')
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute("""
        SELECT question, response, context_docs, signal
        FROM request_log
        WHERE model_slot = 'canary'
          AND signal = 'thumbs_down'
          AND created_at BETWEEN
              TO_TIMESTAMP(%s) - INTERVAL '%s minutes'
              AND TO_TIMESTAMP(%s)
    """, (rollback_at, window_minutes, rollback_at))
    rows = cur.fetchall()
    FIXTURE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    for i, (q, resp, docs, signal) in enumerate(rows):
        path = FIXTURE_DIR / f'rb_{int(rollback_at)}_{i}.json'
        path.write_text(json.dumps({
            'question': q,
            'bad_response': resp,
            'context_docs': docs,
            'signal': signal,
        }, indent=2))
    print(f'Saved {len(rows)} regression fixtures to {FIXTURE_DIR}')

as part of the post-rollback runbook — within 30 minutes of the rollback event while the data is fresh. Add the saved fixtures to the regression suite so they gate future canary advances. A model version that fails on these cases must not be promoted again.
Manual rollback is a fallback. The primary trigger for canary rollback should be automatic: the eval gate script (run by a scheduler) calls
when any metric breaches its threshold. This catches regressions during off-hours without requiring human intervention.

# .github/workflows/canary-watchdog.yml
name: Canary Watchdog

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '*/15 * * * *'   # every 15 minutes

jobs:
  check-and-rollback:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - env:
          REDIS_URL: ${{ secrets.REDIS_URL }}
          DB_URL:    ${{ secrets.DB_URL }}
        run: |
          # Check current canary stage (0-3 based on pct)
          STAGE=$(python -c "from app.flags import flag_store; \
            p=flag_store.get('canary_pct',0); \
            print(0 if p<=0.01 else 1 if p<=0.10 else 2 if p<=0.50 else 3)")
          python scripts/canary_eval_gate.py $STAGE || \
            python scripts/rollback.py 'automated watchdog rollback'
— send a Slack message and (for P1/P2) a PagerDuty alert with the rollback reason, the flag values changed, and the metrics that triggered it.
— compare the failing canary requests with the eval suite. Was there a distribution shift? A prompt injection the guards missed? A new entity type the retriever did not handle?
— if the anomaly was subtle (neg_rate 9.5 % vs a threshold of 10 %), tighten the threshold in
— do not re-attempt the same model version. Fix the underlying regression, bump the version, and start the canary from stage 1 again.

Model weight rollback and prompt rollback are independent operations. If the quality regression traces to a prompt change rather than a model update, rolling back the prompt is sufficient and faster — it does not require touching the model at all. Maintain a
flag so the previous prompt version is always one flag write away:

# Prompt rollback is a flag write — no container restart needed
from app.flags import flag_store

# Roll back to the previous prompt version
def rollback_prompt(reason: str) -> None:
    current = flag_store.get('system_prompt_variant', 'v1')
    versions = flag_store.get('prompt_version_history', ['v1'])

    if len(versions) < 2:
        print('No previous prompt version to roll back to.')
        return

    previous = versions[-2]
    flag_store.set('system_prompt_variant', previous)
    flag_store.set('rollback_reason', f'prompt: {reason}')
    print(f'Prompt rolled back: {current} -> {previous}')
    print('Effect propagates within 30 s (or <1 s with pub/sub).')

# To advance a prompt version safely, append to history first:
def advance_prompt(new_version: str) -> None:
    history = flag_store.get('prompt_version_history', ['v1'])
    history.append(new_version)
    flag_store.set('prompt_version_history', history[-5:])  # keep last 5
    flag_store.set('system_prompt_variant', new_version)

Advance prompt versions through the same flag store and always append to the history before switching traffic. This gives you a safe rollback path for prompt changes, which are often deployed more frequently than model updates.

Rollback scripts are safety-critical code. They must have unit tests that verify the correct flag keys are written in the correct order. A rollback script that writes the wrong key — or worse, writes the model pointer before warming the replacement — is more dangerous than no rollback script at all.

# tests/test_rollback.py
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock, call
from scripts.rollback import flag_rollback
from scripts.model_swap import model_rollback

def test_flag_rollback_sets_correct_keys():
    with patch('scripts.rollback.flag_store') as mock_store:
        flag_rollback('test incident')
        calls = {c.args[0]: c.args[1] for c in mock_store.set.call_args_list}
        assert calls['canary_pct'] == 0.0
        assert calls['new_rag_pipeline'] is False
        assert 'test incident' in calls['rollback_reason']

def test_model_rollback_prewarms_before_swap():
    with patch('scripts.model_swap.warm_model') as mock_warm, \
         patch('scripts.model_swap.flag_store') as mock_store:
        mock_store.get.return_value = 'v2.0.0'
        model_rollback('v1.9.0', 'quality regression')
        # warm_model must be called BEFORE the flag is written
        warm_idx   = [c.args[0] for c in mock_warm.call_args_list].index('v1.9.0')
        set_calls  = [c.args[0] for c in mock_store.set.call_args_list]
        stable_idx = set_calls.index('stable_model')
        assert warm_idx < stable_idx, 'Model must be warmed before flag swap'

Run these tests in CI on every commit to the rollback scripts. Include a staging drill in the team's monthly runbook review: execute the flag rollback against the staging flag store and verify traffic returns to the stable model within 30 s.
— a container redeploy takes 3–10 minutes. Every LLM rollback should be a flag write (< 1 s) or a model swap (< 120 s). If your rollback path involves kubectl or Terraform, you have a readiness gap.

— a cold model load during an active P1 incident takes several minutes while users receive bad outputs. Pre-warming the previous two versions at all times costs GPU memory but eliminates this gap entirely.
Rolling back model and prompt simultaneously
— rolling back two axes at once makes root-cause analysis impossible. Roll back one axis, observe metrics, then roll back the other if needed.
— if the requests that triggered the rollback are not saved as eval cases, the same regression can reappear in the next model update. Every rollback must produce at least one new regression fixture.

— if rollback is only triggered when a human notices a problem, incidents last hours. Automate the canary watchdog to check every 15 minutes and trigger rollback programmatically when thresholds are breached.

LLM rollback has two mechanisms: flag-based (instant, < 1 s with pub/sub) for canary incidents, and model swap (30–120 s pre-warmed) for fully promoted regressions. Classify incidents into P1/P2/P3 to choose the right mechanism without deliberating under pressure. Keep the previous two model versions pre-warmed in GPU memory so rollback never requires a cold load. Automate canary watchdog checks every 15 minutes; save regression fixtures within 30 minutes of every rollback; and never re-promote a version that caused a rollback without fixing the root cause.
